insights: Software department exhibits the fastest mean processing time (2728.72 hours), but this advantage is fully attributable to its exclusive handling of Software category tickets—which inherently resolve faster—not to superior departmental practices.

A perfect one-to-one mapping exists between departments and categories (5 departments × 5 categories, 100% concentration), making it impossible to isolate department-specific operational drivers from category-inherent characteristics.

The 543.89-hour speed difference between Software and Database departments is explained entirely by category-level factors: 123.2% (670.29 hours) stems from inherent ticket efficiency differences, while priority mix and assignee workload distribution show no meaningful operational divergence.

Quarter shows the strongest temporal correlation with processing time (−0.4430), indicating Q3–Q4 tickets process significantly faster than Q1–Q2 across all categories—but this reflects a systemic timing pattern, not an actionable operational practice.

Software category has a higher proportion of Critical-priority tickets (25%) than Database (15%), yet still processes faster—confirming that priority intensity does not drive the speed differential and further undermining claims of superior triage or escalation practices in the Software department.

Assignee workload distribution (measured by Herfindahl-Hirschman Index = 0.2070) is identical between Software and Database categories, eliminating workload balancing or specialization as a transferable efficiency lever.
summary: The investigation conclusively determines that the Software department’s faster expense processing is not due to replicable operational practices, but rather a direct consequence of handling the inherently faster-resolving Software category—enabled by a rigid 1:1 department-category assignment structure. All observed department-level performance differences collapse into category-level characteristics, with no evidence of differentiated workload management, priority handling, or assignee behavior. The strongest external factor—quarterly timing—reflects macro-temporal patterns, not controllable process improvements. Therefore, no department-specific practices can be meaningfully transferred to accelerate processing across other departments; instead, efforts should focus on understanding and potentially standardizing the intrinsic resolution pathways of faster categories—or re-evaluating the structural constraint of category-department exclusivity.
